[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Portugal")]

Public procurement in [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") is governed by the *Código dos Contratos Públicos* or Public Contracts Code (PCC), which has been implemented through the following *Decretos-Leis* ([decree-laws](/wiki/Decree#Portugal "Decree")) and other legislation:

* Decree-Law 18/2008 (29 January 2008)
* Decree-Law 59/2008 (11 September 2008)
* Decree-Law 223/2009 (11 September 2009)
* Decree-Law 278/2009 (20 October 2009)
* Decree-Law 3/2010 (27 April 2010)
* Decree-Law 131/2010 (14 December 2010)
* Law 64-B/2011 (30 December 2012)
* Decree-Law 149/2012 (12 July 2012)
* Decree-Law 214-G/2015 (2 October 2015)
* Decree-Law 111-B/2017 (31 August 2017)[[171]](#cite_note-Portugal-171)

Decree-Law No. 104/2011 (6 October 2011) applies to defence contracts.[[172]](#cite_note-172)

The Administrative Procedural Code, established under decree-law 4/2015 (7 January 2015) also provides for general procedures on administrative matters and the Procedural Code of the Administrative Courts established by Law no. 15/2002 (22 February 2002), amended by Decree-Law 214-G/2015, stipulates procedures for litigation regarding public contracts and procurement practices.[[171]](#cite_note-Portugal-171)

#### Romania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Romania")]

ANAP is the National Agency for Public Procurement in Romania. Public sector tenders in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") are published on the *Sistemul Electronic de Achizitii Publice* (SEAP).[[173]](#cite_note-173) A public procurement reform strategy was developed jointly by the European Commission and the Romanian Government in 2015.[[174]](#cite_note-174)

#### Slovakia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Slovakia")]

Public procurement in [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") is subject to the Law on Public Contracts, which came into effect in September 2015.[[175]](#cite_note-175) Contract opportunities are advertised in the *Slovak Official Journal for Procurement Notices*, as well as in the *[Official Journal of the European Union](/wiki/Official_Journal_of_the_European_Union "Official Journal of the European Union")* when above the threshold values, and a public register of final beneficiaries of companies that win public sector contracts is maintained.[[176]](#cite_note-176) The Public Procurement Regulatory Authority ([Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language"): *l’Úrad pre verejné obstarávanie*) oversees procurement operations.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

#### Slovenia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Slovenia")]

Public procurement in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") is overseen by the Public Procurement Directorate within the Ministry of Public Administration.[[178]](#cite_note-178) The Slovenian Public Procurement Act, the ZJN-3, came into force on 1 April 2016, and covers both public sector and utilities procurement, implementing Directives 2014/24/EU and 2014/25/EU in one piece of legislation.[[179]](#cite_note-179)

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Spain")]

[Spanish](/wiki/Spain "Spain") law on public sector contracts (*Ley 30/2007 de contratos del sector público*, known as the "LCSP") was substantially amended by a new [Law 2/2011 on Sustainable Economy](/w/index.php?title=Law_2/2011_on_Sustainable_Economy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Law 2/2011 on Sustainable Economy (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ley_de_Econom%C3%ADa_Sostenible "es:Ley de Economía Sostenible")] ("LES") following an infringement procedure undertaken by the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), which found that the LCSP "gave contracting authorities a wide, almost unlimited, power to modify essential terms of public contracts after award, in a manner which was not in line with the principles of equal treatment between bidders, non-discrimination and transparency set out in EU public procurement rules".[[180]](#cite_note-180) The [Basque Country](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)") government has issued an "instruction" concerned with inclusion of social and environmental criteria in procurement decision-making.[[138]](#cite_note-bsocial-138): 21 The region's government aims to promote the economic, social and environmental sustainability of the Basque Country through its *Green Procurement and Contracting Program 2030*.[[181]](#cite_note-181)

#### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Sweden")]

The Swedish Competition Authority is responsible for oversight of government procurement in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), having taken over this role from the Board for Public Procurement ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Nämnden för offentlig upphandling*) when it was dissolved in 2007.[[182]](#cite_note-182)[[183]](#cite_note-183)

### Fiji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: Fiji")]

The [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji") Procurement Office was established under Section 4 of the Fiji Procurement Regulations 2010[[184]](#cite_note-FijiRegs-184) and commenced operations on 1 August 2010. The establishment of the Office and the new Fiji Procurement Regulations were a direct result of the re-organisation of the Government Supplies Department by the [Fijian government](/wiki/Government_of_Fiji "Government of Fiji"). The main functions of the Fiji Procurement Office are to regulate and administer the procurement of goods, service and works for the government.[[185]](#cite_note-185) The Government Tender Board is "constituted with authority to approve all procurement of goods, services and works valued at [FJ$](/wiki/Fijian_dollar "Fijian dollar")50,001 and more". Refer [www.fpo.gov.fj](http://www.fpo.gov.fj) for more information[[184]](#cite_note-FijiRegs-184)

### Ghana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: Ghana")]

See also: [Corruption in Ghana](/wiki/Corruption_in_Ghana "Corruption in Ghana")

Public procurement in [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana") is undertaken and overseen by the Public Procurement Authority of Ghana.[[186]](#cite_note-186) The Public Procurement Board is the central body for policy formulation on procurement. The existing Public Procurement Act 2003 (Act 663) was amended by the Public Procurement (Amendment) Act 2016 (Act 914), which came into effect on 1 July 2016.[[187]](#cite_note-187) The Minister of State in Charge of Public Procurement is [Sarah Adwoa Safo](/wiki/Sarah_Adwoa_Safo "Sarah Adwoa Safo").[[188]](#cite_note-188) She has taken a lead role in the fight against [corruption in Ghana](/wiki/Corruption_in_Ghana "Corruption in Ghana"), identifying corruption as "a high-risk activity in the country".[[189]](#cite_note-189)

### Gibraltar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: Gibraltar")]

Government procurement in [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") is managed by the Procurement Office, an independent office of [Her Majesty's Government of Gibraltar](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Government_of_Gibraltar "Her Majesty's Government of Gibraltar") which reports directly to the Financial Secretary.[[190]](#cite_note-190) In 2012 the European Commission raised a concern regarding the United Kingdom's incomplete transposition of the Defence and Security Public Contracts Regulations 2011 into law.[[191]](#cite_note-191) [Gibraltar left the EU](/wiki/Effect_of_Brexit_on_Gibraltar "Effect of Brexit on Gibraltar") on 31 January 2020 at the same time as the UK.

### Guernsey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=69 "Edit section: Guernsey")]

Public procurement opportunities in [Guernsey](/wiki/Guernsey "Guernsey") are advertised on the Channel Islands Procurement Portal, which was launched in April 2008 and is shared with [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey,_Channel_Islands "Jersey, Channel Islands").[[192]](#cite_note-CIPP-192)

### Guyana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: Guyana")]

Public procurement in [Guyana](/wiki/Guyana "Guyana") is overseen by the Public Procurement Commission,[[193]](#cite_note-193) appointed under the Public Procurement Commission Act 2003. Due to lengthy delay in identifying and agreeing commission members, the commission was not appointed until 2016.[[194]](#cite_note-194) The PPC is based in the Queenstown area of [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown,_Guyana "Georgetown, Guyana").[[195]](#cite_note-195) The National Procurement and Tender Administration of Guyana (NPTA), established under section 16(1) of the Procurement Act 2003, undertakes administrative processes for high value governmental tenders.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

### Haiti

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=71 "Edit section: Haiti")]

In 2005, the [Haitian government](/wiki/Haitian_government "Haitian government") formed the National Commission for Public Procurement ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *La Commission Nationale des Marchés Publics*, CNMP), based in [Port-au-Prince](/wiki/Port-au-Prince "Port-au-Prince"), whose tasks are to ensure that competitive bidding takes place for public contracts and to promulgate effective procurement controls in government administration. The commission was established by the Decree of 3 December 2004.[[197]](#cite_note-197) The CNMP publishes lists of awarded public contracts. According to the website [GlobalSecurity.org](/wiki/GlobalSecurity.org "GlobalSecurity.org"), "despite the CNMP's efforts, major public procurement contracts, notably those involving the state electric company EDH, are routinely awarded in a non-competitive fashion", providing significant opportunities for [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Haiti "Corruption in Haiti").[[198]](#cite_note-198)

### Honduras

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=72 "Edit section: Honduras")]

Government procurement in [Honduras](/wiki/Government_of_Honduras "Government of Honduras") is overseen by the National Office of Contracting and Procurement of the State of Honduras (*Oficina Normativa de Contratación y Adquisiciones del Estado*, ONCAE), based in [Tegucigalpa](/wiki/Tegucigalpa "Tegucigalpa"). Honduras has five laws directing public contracting:

* Ley de Contratación del Estado (revised December 2016)
* Ley de Compras Efficientes y Transparentes a través de Medios Electrónicos, *Decreto No. 36-2013*, 1 July 2014[[199]](#cite_note-199)
* Reglamento de la Ley de Compras Efficientes y Transparentes a través de Medios Electrónicos,[[200]](#cite_note-200) whose purpose is to "develop" the law in decree 36–2013
* Reglamento Ley de Contratación del Estado (revised December 2015)
* Reforma al Reglamento de la Ley de Contratación del Estado.[[201]](#cite_note-201)

### Iceland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=73 "Edit section: Iceland")]

Act No. 84/2007 on Public Procurement (2007) has three objectives:

* to ensure the equal treatment of companies during public procurement
* to encourage efficiency in public operations through active competition and
* to promote innovation and development in the public procurement of goods, labour and services.[[202]](#cite_note-fjarmalaraduneyti-202): Article 1 The law applies to "the [Icelandic State](/wiki/Government_of_Iceland "Government of Iceland"), [local authorities](/wiki/Politics_of_Iceland#Administrative_divisions "Politics of Iceland"), their institutions and other public entities" and to "associations formed by one or more of such authorities".[[202]](#cite_note-fjarmalaraduneyti-202): Article 3 An independent Public Procurement Complaints Commission has power to investigate complaints and may declare a contract "ineffective" if its award was not compliant with the legislation.[[202]](#cite_note-fjarmalaraduneyti-202): Articles 91–100

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=74 "Edit section: India")]

See also: [Government e Marketplace](/wiki/Government_e_Marketplace "Government e Marketplace") and [Corruption in India § Tendering processes and awarding contracts](/wiki/Corruption_in_India#Tendering_processes_and_awarding_contracts "Corruption in India")

The government procurement-related disciplines in [India](/wiki/India "India") are governed various Public Procurement Orders and the General Financial Rule. Public Procurement Orders and the General Financial Rule are primarily been taken care of by the Public Procurement Section of [Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)](/wiki/Department_for_Promotion_of_Industry_and_Internal_Trade_(DPIIT) "Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)"), the [Ministry of Commerce and Industry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Commerce_and_Industry_(India) "Ministry of Commerce and Industry (India)") and the Department of Expenditure within the [Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Ministry_of_Finance_(India) "Ministry of Finance (India)") respectively. In 2017, the Public Procurement Order and General Financial Rule was amended by the [government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") to include a [Make In India](/wiki/Make_In_India "Make In India") preference.[[203]](#cite_note-203)[[204]](#cite_note-204)

Emergency procurement powers are available to the [Indian armed forces](/wiki/Indian_Armed_Forces "Indian Armed Forces"), enabling them to meet "urgent operational requirements" without complying with standard procurement procedures.[[205]](#cite_note-205) Under [Indian law](/wiki/Indian_law "Indian law"), an [incumbent](/wiki/Incumbent#Business_usage "Incumbent") contractor who has tendered for an extension contract has a right of first refusal ("ROFR") allowing that contractor to match the lowest bid submitted by a competing company. The case of *[National Highways Authority of India](/wiki/National_Highways_Authority_of_India "National Highways Authority of India") (NHAI) v Gwalior Jhansi Expressway Ltd.* (2018) confirmed that an incumbent contractor who does not submit a tender in accordance with the public body's [notice inviting tenders](/wiki/Invitation_to_tender "Invitation to tender") loses the right of first refusal.[[206]](#cite_note-206)

### Indonesia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=75 "Edit section: Indonesia")]

Indonesia has [observer status](/wiki/Observer_status "Observer status") with respect to the [Government Procurement Agreement](/wiki/Government_Procurement_Agreement "Government Procurement Agreement").[[57]](#cite_note-parties-57) The UK's [Serious Fraud Office](/wiki/Serious_Fraud_Office_(United_Kingdom) "Serious Fraud Office (United Kingdom)") and other regulatory bodies undertook an enquiry into [bribery](/wiki/Bribery "Bribery") payments intended to secure contracts with the [government of Indonesia](/wiki/Government_of_Indonesia "Government of Indonesia") for the supply of [tetraethyl lead](/wiki/Tetraethyllead "Tetraethyllead"), leading to the conviction of four company executives in 2014. The convictions also related to offences concerning [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq").[[207]](#cite_note-207)

### Isle of Man

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=76 "Edit section: Isle of Man")]

The [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") [government](/wiki/Government_of_the_Isle_of_Man "Government of the Isle of Man") spends over £200 million each year on goods, works and services,[[208]](#cite_note-208) promoting competition in procurement under the *Council of Ministers' Procurement Policy for Government*, published in 2017.[[209]](#cite_note-209)

### Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=77 "Edit section: Israel")]

In [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), the Mandatory Tenders Law of 12 March 1992, 5752–1992 (as amended), governs [government](/wiki/Government_of_Israel "Government of Israel") procurement procedures. Oversight of the legislation lies with the [Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Ministry_of_Finance_(Israel) "Ministry of Finance (Israel)") in conjunction with the [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset") Constitution, Law and Justice Committee. The government may, with the approval of the Knesset [Foreign Affairs and Defense Committee](/wiki/Foreign_Affairs_and_Defense_Committee "Foreign Affairs and Defense Committee"), direct that a state or a government corporation may not enter into a contract with a particular foreign country or with a particular foreign supplier for reasons of [foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_Israel "Foreign policy of Israel").[[210]](#cite_note-210)

### Jamaica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=78 "Edit section: Jamaica")]

The [Government of Jamaica](/wiki/Government_of_Jamaica "Government of Jamaica") Procurement Guidelines apply to government procurement in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"),[[211]](#cite_note-211) and the Public Sector Procurement Policy of November 2010 reflects "the government's ... strategy to further reform the public procurement system that is aligned to international best practices and promote fair competition for government contracts".[[212]](#cite_note-Jam-policy-212) Until 1996, Jamaica operated a centralised procurement system coordinated by the Central Supply Division of the Ministry of Finance, and procurement activity was regulated by the Financial Administration (Supplies) Regulations 1963 supplemented by directives from the Ministry of Finance. The Ministry of Finance and the Public Service is now responsible for oversight of procurement policy. A Procurement Policy Implementation Unit was established within the Ministry of Finance in September 1999.[[212]](#cite_note-Jam-policy-212)

The Office of the Contractor-General (OCG), based in [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"), was established in 1983 under the Contractor General Act of that year.[[213]](#cite_note-213) The Contractor General is appointed by the [Governor General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Jamaica "Governor-General of Jamaica"). In December 2008, three members of the procurement committee of the [Jamaica Urban Transit Company](/wiki/Jamaica_Urban_Transit_Company "Jamaica Urban Transit Company") resigned following reports of procurement breaches identified by the Contractor-General, Greg Christie.[[214]](#cite_note-214) The National Contracts Commission (NCC) was established in October 1999.[[212]](#cite_note-Jam-policy-212) Members of the NCC are also appointed by the Governor-General.[[215]](#cite_note-215)

### Jersey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=79 "Edit section: Jersey")]

The [States of Jersey](/wiki/States_of_Jersey "States of Jersey")'s procurement opportunities are advertised on the Channel Islands Procurement Portal, which was launched in April 2008 and is shared with [Guernsey](/wiki/Guernsey "Guernsey").[[192]](#cite_note-CIPP-192)

### Kenya

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=80 "Edit section: Kenya")]

Main article: [Public procurement in Kenya](/wiki/Public_procurement_in_Kenya "Public procurement in Kenya")

Public procurement in [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya") is governed by the Public Procurement and Asset Disposal Act 2015.

### Kosovo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=81 "Edit section: Kosovo")]

The Republic of [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo")'s Public Procurement Regulatory Commission, based in [Pristina](/wiki/Pristina "Pristina"),[[216]](#cite_note-216) is responsible for "the overall development, operation and supervision of the public procurement system in Kosova", subject to regulations imposed by Public Procurement Law of Kosovo No.04/L-042 of 2011.[[217]](#cite_note-217) Law No.04/L-042 was approved by the [Assembly of Kosovo](/wiki/Assembly_of_Kosovo "Assembly of Kosovo") on 29 August 2011, promulgated by the President of the Republic of Kosovo with decree No.DL-032-2011 on 31 August 2011, and published in the official Gazette of the Republic of Kosovo No.18 on 18 September 2011. The amended Public Procurement Law (2017) gives preference to local bidders when the quality and price are comparable to that of foreign bidders.[[218]](#cite_note-dock-218) Kosovo is not a WTO member and is therefore not a signatory to the Government Procurement Agreement.[[218]](#cite_note-dock-218) The [Kosovo Specialist Chambers and Specialist Prosecutor's Office](/wiki/Kosovo_Specialist_Chambers_and_Specialist_Prosecutor%27s_Office "Kosovo Specialist Chambers and Specialist Prosecutor's Office") issues public calls for tender on its own website.[[219]](#cite_note-219)

### Kyrgyz Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=82 "Edit section: Kyrgyz Republic")]

Public sector procurement in the [Kyrgyz Republic](/wiki/Kyrgyz_Republic "Kyrgyz Republic") is regulated by the Law "On Public Procurements" dated 3 April 2015, No. 72; the republic refers to the principles of publicity, openness, legality, and impartiality as critical in relation to suppliers (contractors).[[220]](#cite_note-220)

### Laos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=83 "Edit section: Laos")]

Public procurement in the [Lao People's Democratic Republic](/wiki/Lao_People%27s_Democratic_Republic "Lao People's Democratic Republic") is governed by the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Laos "Prime Minister of Laos")'s Decree on Procurement of Goods, Works, Maintenance and Services No. 03/PM, dated 9 January 2004, and the Implementing Rules and Regulations on Government Procurement of Goods, Works, Maintenance and Services No. 063/PM, dated 12 March 2004.[[221]](#cite_note-Laos-221) Amendments were made to some of the articles of the Implementing Rules and the Decree by Update 0861/MOF of 5 May 2009.[[222]](#cite_note-222) Procurement activities are overseen by the Procurement Monitoring Office (PrMO) within the Ministry of Finance.[[221]](#cite_note-Laos-221)

### Liberia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=84 "Edit section: Liberia")]

Government procurement in [Liberia](/wiki/Liberia "Liberia") is governed by the Public Procurement Act. The Public Procurement and Concessions Commission (PPCC) was established in 2005 to "regulate all forms of Public Procurement and Concessions and provide for institutional structures for public procurement and concessions".[[223]](#cite_note-PPCCFAQ-223) The PPCC operates an online Vendors' Register.[[224]](#cite_note-224) Procurement is decentralised, but the [Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Ministry_of_Finance,_Liberia "Ministry of Finance, Liberia") is required "to take part in the negotiations and signing of contracts over US$250,000" and such contracts must "be attested to by the Ministry of Justice".[[223]](#cite_note-PPCCFAQ-223)

### Liechtenstein

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=85 "Edit section: Liechtenstein")]

[Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein") is a member of the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area") (EEA) and subject to Annex XVI (Procurement) to the EEA Agreement. The Annex provides that its references to [ILO Conventions](/wiki/ILO_Conventions "ILO Conventions") do not apply to Liechtenstein, but equivalent standards on labour conditions are to be applied.[[225]](#cite_note-225)

### Malaysia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=86 "Edit section: Malaysia")]

Malaysia has [observer status](/wiki/Observer_status "Observer status") with respect to the [Government Procurement Agreement](/wiki/Government_Procurement_Agreement "Government Procurement Agreement").[[57]](#cite_note-parties-57)

### Maldives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=87 "Edit section: Maldives")]

Government procurement in the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives") is subject to the Public Finance Law (Law No. 3/2006) and chapter 10 of the Public Finance Regulation. The approval of the National Tender Board is required before contracts in excess of [MVR](/wiki/Maldivian_rufiyaa "Maldivian rufiyaa") 2.5m can be awarded.[[226]](#cite_note-226)

### Mali

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=88 "Edit section: Mali")]

Public procurement in [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali") is overseen by the Autorité de Régulation des Marchés Publics et des Délégations de Service Public,[[227]](#cite_note-227) based in [Bamako](/wiki/Bamako "Bamako").

### Mauritius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=89 "Edit section: Mauritius")]

For several cases of corruption in government procurement in Mauritius, see [Corruption in Mauritius](/wiki/Corruption_in_Mauritius "Corruption in Mauritius").

Public bodies in the [Republic of Mauritius](/wiki/Republic_of_Mauritius "Republic of Mauritius") use the country's *Public Procurement Portal* to post information relating to their public procurement activities.[[228]](#cite_note-228) Public Procurement Regulations date from 2008, with a number of additional regulations subsequently adopted.[[229]](#cite_note-229)

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=90 "Edit section: Mexico")]

Public procurement is included in Article 134 of the [Mexican Constitution](/wiki/Mexican_Constitution "Mexican Constitution"). Article 134 is implemented by the Law of Public Sector Acquisitions, Leasing and Services ("Acquisition Law") and the Law of Public Works and Related Services ("Public Work Law"). At a local level, each of the 31 [states](/wiki/States_of_Mexico "States of Mexico") and the [Federal District](/wiki/Federal_District_(Mexico) "Federal District (Mexico)") has different public procurement laws.[[230]](#cite_note-230)

### Moldova

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=91 "Edit section: Moldova")]

The [Republic of Moldova](/wiki/Republic_of_Moldova "Republic of Moldova") ratified the [Government Procurement Agreement](/wiki/Government_procurement_agreement "Government procurement agreement") on 14 June 2016.[[231]](#cite_note-231)

### Montenegro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=92 "Edit section: Montenegro")]

Montenegro joined the Agreement on Government Procurement in 2015 after approval was granted on 29 October 2014.[[232]](#cite_note-monz-232) Exclusions apply in respect of

* procurement of agricultural products for agricultural support programmes and human [food aid](/wiki/Food_aid "Food aid") programmes
* procurement of [broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting") material by broadcasters, and contracts for broadcasting time, and
* some aspects of procurement connected with the provision of [drinking water](/wiki/Drinking_water "Drinking water"), energy, transport and the postal sector.[[233]](#cite_note-233)

### Morocco

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=93 "Edit section: Morocco")]

[Morocco](/wiki/Government_of_Morocco "Government of Morocco")'s National Commission for Public Procurement (CNCP) was established "to oversee public procurement, control public spending and guarantee the principles of transparency and parity in the development and execution of contracts between competitors", with a role also in handling complaints regarding procurement actions.[[234]](#cite_note-234)

### Mozambique

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=94 "Edit section: Mozambique")]

Decree no. 5/2016, *Public Procurement Regulations* governs public procurement in [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique").[[235]](#cite_note-235)

### Namibia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=95 "Edit section: Namibia")]

[Namibia](/wiki/Government_of_Namibia "Government of Namibia")'s Procurement Management Unit is based within the Ministry of Finance & Public Enterprises, located in [Windhoek](/wiki/Windhoek "Windhoek"). Bid opportunities are listed online by the unit.[[236]](#cite_note-236)

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=96 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

The [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") Government Procurement Branch of the [Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment](/wiki/Ministry_of_Business,_Innovation_and_Employment "Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment") is responsible for the Government Procurement Rules, Government Rules of Sourcing[[237]](#cite_note-sour-237) and Principles of Government Procurement.[[238]](#cite_note-238) The aim of the Government Rules of Sourcing is to "support good practice for procurement planning, approaching the supplier community and contracting".[[237]](#cite_note-sour-237) The 66 rules were initially introduced in 2013. The principles apply to all governmental procurement activity but the rules only apply to projects or purchases exceeding $100,000 or construction projects valued over $10 million.[[237]](#cite_note-sour-237)

Purchasers of certain common goods or services are required to use "All-of-Government contracts" (AoG) established by the Government Procurement Branch, overseen by the Procurement Functional Leader and managed by appointed procurement Centres of Expertise. Where a public agency wishes to opt out of the use of an AoG contract it must obtain the approval of approval the Procurement Functional Leader: if the agency and the Procurement Functional Leader fail to agree on an opt-out, the [State Services Commissioner](/wiki/State_Services_Commissioner "State Services Commissioner") will decide.[[239]](#cite_note-239)

[![Blavatnik School of Government](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/BSG_LOGO_for_word.jpg/250px-BSG_LOGO_for_word.jpg)](/wiki/File:BSG_LOGO_for_word.jpg "Blavatnik School of Government")

Blavatnik School of Government

New Zealand joined the Agreement on Government Procurement in 2015 after approval was granted on 29 October 2014.[[232]](#cite_note-monz-232) The second International Civil Service Effectiveness Index, published in April 2019 by the [Blavatnik School of Government](/wiki/Blavatnik_School_of_Government "Blavatnik School of Government") at the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), ranked New Zealand as the top country for Government Procurement Effectiveness. The procurement indicator was a new addition to this index, not present in the previous 2017 index. The indicator covered both procurement systems and procurement practices. The report authors identified that New Zealand's excellence lay in "the extent of e-procurement functions within its overall procurement system; the role of its central purchasing body; and the extent to which policies are in place to enable small and medium-sized enterprises (SME) to take part in central government procurement".[[240]](#cite_note-240)

### Nigeria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=97 "Edit section: Nigeria")]

Nigeria is a [federal](/wiki/Federation "Federation") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic") comprising [36 states](/wiki/States_of_Nigeria "States of Nigeria") and the [Federal Capital Territory](/wiki/Federal_Capital_Territory,_Nigeria "Federal Capital Territory, Nigeria").[[241]](#cite_note-241) See [Rivers State Bureau on Public Procurement](/wiki/Rivers_State_Bureau_on_Public_Procurement "Rivers State Bureau on Public Procurement") as an example of a regulatory body in one of the states.

### North Macedonia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=98 "Edit section: North Macedonia")]

[North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia") has ratified the Government Procurement Agreement and acceded to membership on 30 October 2023.[[242]](#cite_note-242)

### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=99 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

Government procurement in [Pakistan](/wiki/Islamic_Republic_of_Pakistan "Islamic Republic of Pakistan") is overseen by the [Public Procurement Regulatory Authority](/wiki/Public_Procurement_Regulatory_Authority "Public Procurement Regulatory Authority") (PPRA), an autonomous body based in [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad") which was established by the Public Procurement Regulatory Authority Ordinance of May 2002. The PPRA is responsible for issuing regulations and procedures for public procurement undertaken by federal level public sector organisations. Its brief is to improve the governance, management, transparency, accountability and quality of Pakistan's public procurement. The PPRA also monitors other public sector agencies' procurement activity.[[243]](#cite_note-243) The PPRA Board consists of six ministerial appointments from [central government departments](/wiki/Government_of_Pakistan "Government of Pakistan"), three private members and the Authority's managing director.[[244]](#cite_note-244) Pakistan has [observer status](/wiki/Observer_status "Observer status") with respect to the [Government Procurement Agreement](/wiki/Government_Procurement_Agreement "Government Procurement Agreement").[[57]](#cite_note-parties-57)

### Peru

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=100 "Edit section: Peru")]

[Peruvian](/wiki/Peru "Peru") public procurement law is set out in the Public Procurement General Act (Law N° 32069) of 2024.[[245]](#cite_note-245) Previous legislation included the Government Procurement Act (approved by Law No. 30225 ), Government Procurement Act (approved by Legislative Decree No. 1017) and the Regulation of the Government Procurement Act (approved by Supreme Decree No. 184-2008-EF). Peruvian [President](/wiki/President_of_Peru "President of Peru") [Pedro Pablo Kuczynski](/wiki/Pedro_Pablo_Kuczynski "Pedro Pablo Kuczynski") resigned on 21 March 2018 following allegations that public works contracts had been [corruptly](/wiki/Corruption_in_Peru "Corruption in Peru") awarded to [Brazilian](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") conglomerate [Odebrecht](/wiki/Odebrecht "Odebrecht").[[246]](#cite_note-246)

### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=101 "Edit section: Philippines")]

Public sector procurement in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") is required to follow the [Government Procurement Reform Act](/wiki/Government_Procurement_Reform_Act "Government Procurement Reform Act") of 2003. The [Philippine government](/wiki/Government_of_the_Philippines "Government of the Philippines") requested [observer status](/wiki/Observer_status "Observer status") with respect to the [Government Procurement Agreement](/wiki/Government_Procurement_Agreement "Government Procurement Agreement") on 6 May 2019 and its request was accepted by the WTO Committee on Government Procurement on 26 June 2019, the Philippines confirming that "its government was ... taking steps to create a transparent, open and fair procurement system, founded on a sound legal framework, which includes initiatives to open procurement to foreign suppliers".[[247]](#cite_note-247)

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=102 "Edit section: Russia")]

Main article: [Government procurement in Russia](/wiki/Government_procurement_in_Russia "Government procurement in Russia")

[Russian](/wiki/Russia "Russia") Federal Law N44-ФЗ of 5 April 2013 requires all federal, regional and municipal government customers to publish all information about government tenders, auctions and other purchase procedures on special public government websites.

### Rwanda

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=103 "Edit section: Rwanda")]

In [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"), the public procurement process is managed on a daily basis by an autonomous organ, the Rwanda Public Procurement Authority (RPPA),[[248]](#cite_note-248) which operates under the Ministry of Finance and Economic Planning (MINECOFIN). Public procurement is regulated by the Law N°12/2007 of 27 March 2007 on public procurement[[249]](#cite_note-249) which was modified and complemented by the Law N°05/2013 of 13 February 2013.[[250]](#cite_note-250) The law is implemented by a Ministerial Order N°001/14/10/TC of 19 February 2014 establishing Regulations on Public Procurement, Standard Bidding Documents and Standard Contracts.[[251]](#cite_note-251)

Rwanda has a decentralized public procurement system whereby procuring entities (central government organs, local government entities, government projects, commissions, public institutions, parastatals, agencies or any other government entity charged by the Chief Budget Manager to manage public funds) have the power to conduct directly their public procurement process. The main mission of RPPA is (1) to process the establishment and improvement of public procurement legal framework, (2) provide public procurement legal advisory services, (3) conduct audit and monitoring of public procurement activities carried out by procuring entities (tender award and contract management) and (4) build the capacity of public officials involved in public procurement activities.[[252]](#cite_note-252)

The public procurement system in Rwanda is governed by 6 fundamental principles namely (1) transparency, (2) competition,(3) economy, (4) efficiency, (5) fairness and (6) accountability.[[253]](#cite_note-253) In the national system, bidders have the right to appeal against public procurement procedures they may think were not conducted appropriately. In that connection, the legal framework provides for the Independent Review Panels at National Level (National Independent Review Panel) and at District Level (Independent Review Panel at District Level).[[254]](#cite_note-254) The Independent Review Panels are composed of members from the Private Sector, Civil Society and the Public Sector, and the members from the Public Sector cannot form the majority of members of the Panel. The Independent Review Panel at National Level is under the supervision of the Minister of Finance and Economic Planning whereas the Independent Review Panel at District Level is under the supervision of the District Council. Rwanda introduced an e-procurement system in 2016 In order to make the procurement sector a profession in Rwanda, there is an Association of Procurement Professionals which was established by the Law N°011/2016 of 2 May 2016.[[255]](#cite_note-255)

### Serbia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=104 "Edit section: Serbia")]

The [Serbian](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") law on public procurement came into effect on 1 April 2013, replacing the previous legislation enacted in 2008. A particular concern for Serbia's [legislators](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Serbia) "National Assembly (Serbia)") was dealing with [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Serbia "Corruption in Serbia") in government procurement: the Law requires Serbia's Public Procurement Office, which oversees procurement, to draft a plan for combating corruption in public procurement procedures, and contracting authorities with an estimated annual value of public procurement in excess of one billion [dinars](/wiki/Dinar "Dinar") (8.9m Euros) to adopt an internal plan for preventing corruption.[[256]](#cite_note-256) The Public Procurement Office is based in [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade"). The role of the Republic Commission for the Protection of Rights in Public Procurement Procedures, established in 2002, is to protect the rights of bidders during procurement exercises.[[257]](#cite_note-257)

The Regulation on Mandatory Elements of Tender Documents in Public Procurement Procedures and Way to Prove Fulfilment of Requirements[[258]](#cite_note-258) prescribes a model contract as a mandatory element of every set of tender documents, except when a negotiated procedure is being conducted or where a loan is being procured as a financial service.[[259]](#cite_note-259) In 2016, the EU funded a programme of support for "further improvement of Public Procurement system in Serbia", as part of the EU's pre-accession assistance programme.[[260]](#cite_note-260) There is no current target date for Serbia to [join the EU](/wiki/Accession_of_Serbia_to_the_European_Union "Accession of Serbia to the European Union").[[261]](#cite_note-261)

### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=105 "Edit section: Singapore")]

See also: [GeBIZ](/wiki/GeBIZ "GeBIZ")

[Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")'s [Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Ministry_of_Finance_(Singapore) "Ministry of Finance (Singapore)") is responsible for the Government Procurement policy framework, which regulates how government agencies undertake procurement.[[262]](#cite_note-Singapore-262) Singapore's whole-of-government (WOG) procurement function represents an ambition to draw together all Government procurement policies, practices, systems and workforce development under the oversight of the Government Procurement Function Office.[[263]](#cite_note-263) In 2014, the [Public Accounts Committee](/wiki/Public_Accounts_Committee "Public Accounts Committee") of the [Parliament of Singapore](/wiki/Parliament_of_Singapore "Parliament of Singapore") criticised the state of government procurement in Singapore, identifying a number of irregularities in procurement procedures including:

* weak rationales invoked when [waiving](/wiki/Waiver "Waiver") competitive processes;
* allowing some bidders to amend their tenders after tenders had closed;
* not disclosing evaluation criteria to bidders within tender documentation;
* improper procedures for tender evaluation;
* lax oversight and monitoring of outsourced projects.[[264]](#cite_note-264)

[GeBIZ](/wiki/GeBIZ "GeBIZ") is a [Government-to-business](/wiki/Government-to-business "Government-to-business") (G2B) [Public eProcurement](/wiki/Public_eProcurement "Public eProcurement") business centre where suppliers can conduct [electronic commerce](/wiki/Electronic_commerce "Electronic commerce") with the [Singaporean](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") Government. All of the public sector's invitations for quotations and tenders (except for security-sensitive contracts) are posted on GeBIZ.[[262]](#cite_note-Singapore-262) Suppliers can search for government procurement opportunities, retrieve relevant procurement documentations and submit their bids online.

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=106 "Edit section: South Africa")]

Section 217 of the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_South_Africa "Constitution of South Africa") of the [Republic of South Africa](/wiki/Republic_of_South_Africa "Republic of South Africa"), 1996 (Act 108 of 1996) provides the basis for government procurement:

:   (1) When an organ of state in the national, provincial or local sphere of Government, or any other institution identified in national legislation, contracts for goods or services, it must do so in accordance with a system which is fair, equitable, transparent, competitive and cost effective.
:   (2) Subsection (1) does not prevent the organs of state or institutions referred to in that subsection from implementing a procurement policy providing for –

    :   (a) categories of preference in the allocation of contracts; and
    :   (b) the protection or advancement of persons, or categories of persons, disadvantaged by unfair discrimination.
:   (3) National legislation must prescribe a framework within which the policy referred to in subsection (2) must be implemented.

In Subsection (3), the previous words "may be implemented" were amended to "must be implemented" by section 6 of the [Constitution Seventh Amendment Act of 2001](/wiki/Seventh_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_South_Africa "Seventh Amendment of the Constitution of South Africa").[[265]](#cite_note-265) The Public Finance Management Act 1999 also refers to the duty of the Accounting Officer of a department to have and to maintain an appropriate procurement and supply system which is "fair, equitable, transparent, competitive and cost effective".[[266]](#cite_note-KwaZuluNatal-266) To help prevent corruption, a Central Tender Board was established in 2014.[[267]](#cite_note-267)

### South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=107 "Edit section: South Korea")]

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit), over 90% of all government procurement in South Korea was undertaking using the country's centrally administered Korea ON-line E-Procurement System (KONEPS),[[268]](#cite_note-268): 3 which had been established in 2002. The [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") describes KONEPS as "an integrated e-procurement system which contributes substantially to the efficiency, effectiveness and integrity of public procurement in Korea".[[269]](#cite_note-269)

South Korean [Ministry of National Defense](/wiki/Ministry_of_National_Defense_(South_Korea) "Ministry of National Defense (South Korea)") contracting was highlighted in a [California Supreme Court](/wiki/California_Supreme_Court "California Supreme Court") case in 2003, *Korea Supply Company v Lockhead Martin*, when Korea Supply accused Lockhead of [unfair commercial practices](/wiki/Unfair_commercial_practices "Unfair commercial practices") including bribing Korean officials.[[270]](#cite_note-270)

### Suriname

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=108 "Edit section: Suriname")]

Government procurement in [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname") takes place on the basis of open tenders. Participants in a tendering procedure must hold a valid business license and must be registered with the Suriname Chamber of Commerce and Industry (KKF). Suriname is not a signatory to the WTO [Government Procurement Agreement](/wiki/Government_procurement_agreement "Government procurement agreement").[[271]](#cite_note-271)

### Tajikistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=109 "Edit section: Tajikistan")]

[Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") is not a signatory to the WTO [Government Procurement Agreement](/wiki/Government_procurement_agreement "Government procurement agreement"), but the [US Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") has noted that the country has made a commitment to initiate accession to the agreement as part of its WTO accession protocol.[[272]](#cite_note-272)

### Tanzania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=110 "Edit section: Tanzania")]

Public procurement in the [United Republic of Tanzania](/wiki/United_Republic_of_Tanzania "United Republic of Tanzania") is overseen by the country's Public Procurement Regulatory Authority (PPRA), which was established under the terms of the Public Procurement Act in 2004. The objectives of the PPRA are:

> to ensure the application of fair, competitive, transparent, non-discriminatory and value for money procurement standards and practices; set standards for the public procurement systems in United Republic of Tanzania; monitor compliance of procuring entities; and build, in collaboration with Public Procurement Policy Division and other relevant professional bodies, procurement capacity in the United Republic.[[273]](#cite_note-tanz-273)

The Public Procurement Act 2004 has been superseded by the Public Procurement Act 2011.[[273]](#cite_note-tanz-273)

### Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=111 "Edit section: Ukraine")]

The [Ministry of Economic Development and Trade (Ukraine)](/wiki/Ministry_of_Economic_Development_and_Trade_(Ukraine) "Ministry of Economic Development and Trade (Ukraine)") is an executive authority in charge of coordination of procurement of goods, works and services for public funds.[[274]](#cite_note-274) The Law "On public procurement" is one of the core legislative bases of the procurement regulations. It made electronic public procurement procedures and use of e-procurement system [Prozorro](/wiki/Prozorro "Prozorro") mandatory for all procuring entities after August 2016.[[275]](#cite_note-275) Ukraine joined the [Government Procurement Agreement](/wiki/Government_procurement_agreement "Government procurement agreement") in March 2016.[[276]](#cite_note-276)

### United Arab Emirates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=112 "Edit section: United Arab Emirates")]

Federal government procurement within the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") is governed by *Cabinet Resolution No. 32 of 2014 on Federal Government Procurement Regulation and Storehouse Management in Federal Government*, which applies to all supply, works and services purchasing undertaken by the federal government and the federal ministries and governmental agencies (except the Ministry of Defence), and also to independent federal entities such as the General Authority for Civil Aviation, Emirates Real Estate Corporation, FEWA, ESCA, Insurance Authority, Emirates Post Group Holding, National Transport Authority, Telecommunications Regulatory Authority, [UAE University](/wiki/UAE_University "UAE University") and [Zayed University](/wiki/Zayed_University "Zayed University").[[277]](#cite_note-Lysenko-277) UAE Federal Decree No. 12 of the Deputy Supreme Commander of the Armed Forces (1986) applies to armed forces procurement.[[278]](#cite_note-278) Conditional preferential treatment is afforded under Resolution 32 to corporate suppliers whose capital does not exceed [AED](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_dirham "United Arab Emirates dirham") 10 million and in which the UAE national [shareholding](/wiki/Shareholder "Shareholder") is not less than 51%, and to facilities which are financed by SMEs-supporting funds and governed by federal or local law.[[277]](#cite_note-Lysenko-277)

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=113 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main article: [Government procurement in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Government_procurement_in_the_United_Kingdom "Government procurement in the United Kingdom")

At around £290 billion every year, public sector procurement accounts for around a third of all public expenditure in the UK.[[279]](#cite_note-279) On 24 February 2025, the [Procurement Act 2023](/wiki/Procurement_Act_2023 "Procurement Act 2023") took effect.[[280]](#cite_note-280) Previously, EU-based laws such as the Public Contracts Regulations 2015, Part 3 of the [Small Business, Enterprise and Employment Act 2015](/wiki/Small_Business,_Enterprise_and_Employment_Act_2015 "Small Business, Enterprise and Employment Act 2015"),[[281]](#cite_note-281) and (in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland")) the Public Contracts (Scotland) Regulations of 2015 [[282]](#cite_note-282) and 2016[[283]](#cite_note-283) continued to apply to government procurement.

### United Nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=114 "Edit section: United Nations")]

In 2021, the 41 [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") [organizations](/wiki/List_of_specialized_agencies_of_the_United_Nations "List of specialized agencies of the United Nations") between them spent US$29.6 billion on purchasing goods and services. The UN's [Office for Project Services](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_for_Project_Services "United Nations Office for Project Services") supports UN procurement and publishes an annual analysis of the combined UN expenditure and key trends in UN procurement.[[284]](#cite_note-284)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=115 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Government procurement in the United States](/wiki/Government_procurement_in_the_United_States "Government procurement in the United States")

Government procurement and government contracting by public authorities in the United States accounts for about US$7 trillion annually;[[22]](#cite_note-Prieß:_Global_Overview-22) the central purchasing agency is the [General Services Administration](/wiki/General_Services_Administration "General Services Administration") (GSA). Federal procurement is governed by the [Federal Acquisition Regulation](/wiki/Federal_Acquisition_Regulation "Federal Acquisition Regulation") which is intended by [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") to ensure that taxpayer money is used wisely, in ways that benefit the public or nation as a whole.[[285]](#cite_note-285)

*FedBizOpps* operated until 2019 as a source of information on government contracts. This is now [SAM.gov](/wiki/SAM.gov "SAM.gov").[[286]](#cite_note-286) Public announcements of awards allow for several exemptions, including contracts less than $3.5 million.[[287]](#cite_note-287) Historically, the procurement data has been criticized for deficiencies leading to a number of reforms.[[288]](#cite_note-Halchin2013-288) In 2013, eight legacy databases were merged into a single system called "[System for Award Management](/wiki/System_for_Award_Management "System for Award Management")" (SAM), where companies interested in doing business with the federal government may register their interest.[[289]](#cite_note-289)

Contracts are not posted online, although two agencies have explored the possibility.[[288]](#cite_note-Halchin2013-288) In January 2014, the [Office of Inspector General](/wiki/Office_of_Inspector_General_(United_States) "Office of Inspector General (United States)") at [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") released a report criticizing the agency's lack of strategic sourcing.[[290]](#cite_note-290) Because IT departments were spending autonomously, NASA spent $25.7 million on similar purchases.[[291]](#cite_note-291) The National Institute of Governmental Purchasing and the [Federal Acquisition Institute](/w/index.php?title=Federal_Acquisition_Institute&action=edit&redlink=1 "Federal Acquisition Institute (page does not exist)") are active in procurement certification and training. A specialized program in procurement law in the United States is located at [The George Washington University Law School](/wiki/The_George_Washington_University_Law_School "The George Washington University Law School").

### Vatican City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=116 "Edit section: Vatican City")]

Purchasing is overseen by the [Secretariat for the Economy](/wiki/Secretariat_for_the_Economy_(Holy_See) "Secretariat for the Economy (Holy See)"), which is responsible for setting purchasing policies and procedures, while responsibility for expenditure is devolved to individual [dicasteries](/wiki/Dicastery "Dicastery") and administrations.[[292]](#cite_note-292) Rules on "transparency, control and competition in the procedures for awarding public contracts of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") and [Vatican City State](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City")" were published on 19 May 2020 and amended on 16 January 2024.[[293]](#cite_note-293)

### Vietnam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=117 "Edit section: Vietnam")]

A directive was issued in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") in April 2010 concerning the use of domestic products and materials and on public contracts for these products, where they are financed with state funds.[[85]](#cite_note-eesc-85): 6

### World Bank Group

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=118 "Edit section: World Bank Group")]

The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") functions like a cooperative owned by its 189 member countries.[[294]](#cite_note-294) Its own procurement activities are handled at a corporate level,[[295]](#cite_note-295) and its Procurement Framework, approved in July 2015 and operational since July 2016, applies to projects and programmes which are supported with World Bank funds. The Procurement Framework has four key principles:

* Needs and risks of a project are analyzed through a Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD). This analysis enables the borrower to have a strategy on how best to engage with bidders. The analysis ensures that procurement processes are fit for purpose, allow choice, and are appropriate to the size, value, and risk of the project.
* Value for Money is a core procurement principle in all procurements financed by the World Bank. This means the focus is on bids that provide the best overall value for money, taking into account quality, cost, and other factors as needed, rather than a focus on the lowest evaluated compliant bid.
* The approach to resolving procurement-related complaints has the capacity to promptly respond to any concerns during the procurement process. There is a standstill period -a pause between identifying who should win the contract and actually awarding them the contract- so that other bidders can voice any concerns before a contract is actually legally formed and awarded.
* The World Bank is more involved in contract management of procurements with high value and high risk to ensure the best possible outcomes and that problems are resolved quickly.[[296]](#cite_note-296)

### Zambia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Government_procurement&action=edit&section=119 "Edit section: Zambia")]

Public procurement in [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia") is governed by the Public Procurement Act No. 12 of 2008 and the Public Procurement Regulations of 2011. Prior to 2008, public procurement was governed by the Zambia National Tender Board Act, Act No. 30 of 1982.[[297]](#cite_note-297) The CEO of the
Zambia National Tender Board is appointed by the [President](/wiki/President_of_Zambia "President of Zambia").[[298]](#cite_note-298)
